rulings.Case Title: WILLIAM Y. MOORES MD VS. MEDICAL BOARD OF CALIFORNIA, DIVISION OF MEDICAL et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 3RD AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, July 16, 2014, Line 12: DEFENDANTS LINDA WHITNEY, SHARON LEVINE, M.D., and MEDICAL BOARD OF CALIFORNIA, DIVISION OF MEDICAL QUALITY'S DEMURRER TO 3RD AMENDED COMPLAINT. Sustained without leave to amend. (CCP sec. 430.10(e).) All requests for judicial notice are granted. The Third Amended Complaint ("TAC") does not adequately allege that Defendant's alleged violation of Bus. and Prof. Code sec. 803.1 proximately caused Plaintiff's damages. An exhibit attached to the Second Amended Complaint ("SAC"), and therefore an adopted admission of Plaintiff, unambiguously states that Plaintiff was not hired because he "would have to be proctored should [he] resume doing procedures." (Defendant's Request for Judicial Notice, Ex. A., SAC, Ex. 28; see Barnett v. Fireman's Fund Ins. Co. (2001) 90 Cal.App.4th 500, 505, "we rely on and accept as true the contents of the exhibits and treat as surplusage the pleader's allegations as to the legal effects of the exhibits.") Further, Plaintiff requests that the Court take judicial notice of an email in support of his opposition to the demurrer that shows that Defendant spoke with the potential employer "to clarify the status of [Plaintiff's] California medical license." (Plaintiff's Request for Judicial Notice, Ex. C, SAC, Ex. 27.) Therefore, the allegations that Defendant failed to provide information about the status of Plaintiff's license, or the allegations that Defendant's failure to provide information proximately caused Plaintiff's damages, are contradicted by the judicially noticed exhibits. The demurrer is sustained without leave to amend in light of the fact that Plaintiff has already had an opportunity to amend this cause of action. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4:00 p.m. stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if in pro per on the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: WILLIAM Y. MOORES MD VS. MEDICAL BOARD OF CALIFORNIA, DIVISION OF MEDICAL et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE 3RD AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, July 16, 2014, Line 12: DEFENDANTS LINDA WHITNEY, SHARON LEVINE, M.D., and MEDICAL BOARD OF CALIFORNIA, DIVISION OF MEDICAL QUALITY'S MOTION TO STRIKE 3RD AMENDED COMPLAINT. Denied. At the hearing on Defendant's Demurrer to the Second Amended Complaint, Defendant's counsel stated on the record that he would not object to Plaintiff asserting another theory of liability. Further, it serves judicial economy to allow this new cause of action. If Plaintiff were to request leave to amend, it would almost certainly be granted since no trial date has been set yet. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4:00 p.m. stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: JOANNA FULL VS. BAY AREA RAPID TRANSIT ("BART") et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn To Compel Compliance With Deposition Subpoena; Request For Order Awarding Monetary Sanctions Against California Pacific Medical Center In The Amount Of $8,330.50
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, July 16, 2014, Line 4: DEFENDANT BAY AREA RAPID TRANSIT ("BART")'S Motion To Compel Compliance With Deposition Subpoena; Request For Order Awarding Monetary Sanctions Against California Pacific Medical Center In The Amount Of $8,330.50. The motion is granted. Howell v. Hamilton Meats (2011) 52 Cal. 4th 541, 555, indicates that the discovery regarding the reasonable value of the medical services is appropriate. The medical expenses must be incurred and reasonable. The court understands that the parties have agreed to stipulate to a protective order. The court denies monetary sanctions because all parties acted with substantial justification. The court will not provide a court reporter for this discovery hearing. If any party wishes a reporter, the parties must meet and confer and agree on a single reporter. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: BRAD D SANDERS VS. EDWARD ZUSMAN, AN INDIVIDUAL ATTORNEY LICENSED TO et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Brad Sanders Motion For An In Camera Review Of A Document Redacted In The Presence Of This Court, Or In The Alternative For An Order Shortening Time For Service Of A Motion
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Wednesday, July 16, 2014, Line 2, Plaintiff Brad Sanders' Motion For An In Camera Review Of A Document Redacted In The Presence Of This Court, Or In The Alternative For An Order Shortening Time For Service Of A Motion Pro Tem Judge Tom Cohen, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion is denied. The context of the email string indicates that the redacted name is unrelated to plaintiff's case. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to tom@thomascohen.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: NANCY BARSOTTI VS. THE CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, July 16, 2014, Line 13: DEFENDANTS THE CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO, THE SAN FRANCISCO FIRE DEPARTMENT, and THE SAN FRANCISCO FIRE COMMISSION'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. Overruled. Liberally construing the complaint, Defendant David Johnson was performing operational acts in facilitating and overseeing the examination process and therefore does not enjoy immunity under Gov. Code sec. 820.2. The Complaint pleads that Johnson was implementing the decisions of the Chief of the Fire Department. (Complaint, pars. 54, 60, 64.) Gov. Code sec. 822.2 immunity does not bar the 5th cause of action because the Complaint sufficiently pleads actual fraud. (Complaint, pars. 268-283.) Defendants' argument about summary adjudication being granted in the Danner case is irrelevant. A demurrer does not consider whether Plaintiffs can actually prove their causes of action, only whether they are adequately pled. Worker's Compensation Exclusivity does not bar the negligence claims because Plaintiffs allege economic injuries that are not compensable under the Worker's Compensation scheme. (Complaint, pars. 295, 306; Singh v. Southland Stone USA (2010) 186 Cal.App.4th 338, 365-66, "only injuries to a worker's person, as opposed to his property, are compensable.") The Complaint adequately provides a statutory basis for the sixth and seventh causes of action. (C.A. v. William S. Hart Union High School Dist. (2012) 53 Cal.4th 861, 868, "the general rule is that an employee of a public entity is liable for his torts to the same extent as a private person (§ 820, subd. (a)) and the public entity is vicariously liable for any injury which its employee causes (§ 815.2, subd. (a)) to the same extent as a private employer (§ 815, subd. (b)).") All requests for judicial notice are granted. The Court did not consider arguments raised for the first time in reply. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)
